analysis.quote: We kicked off mock draft 3.0 by selecting a surprise faller in Ohio State WR Carnell Tate! Tate is known for his smooth route running, strong hands, and ability to stretch the field vertically. He caught 52 passes for 873 yards and 10 touchdowns in his final season with the Buckeyes, showcasing both speed and body control that NFL teams drool over. We've now added a true deep threat to pair with Malik Willis, and we couldn't be more pumped about Tate's size and playmaking ability opening up the field for the new-look Dolphins passing game.

Next up in our mock, we went after Oklahoma edge rusher R Mason Thomas! Thomas is a speed rusher with a ton of bend and quickness off the edge. He totaled 12 sacks and 20 tackles for loss in his final season, showing he can get after the quarterback in a hurry. This pick gives the Dolphins a nasty, high-upside pass rusher to pair with Chop Robinson and Josh Uche, and we're hyped to see how he shakes up the new defensive front.

We couldn't pass on Pitt linebacker Kyle Louis in this mock! Louis is a sideline-to-sideline monster who absolutely turned heads at the Senior Bowl. Known for his instinctive reads and aggressive tackling, he racked up 95 tackles and 5 sacks in his final season with the Panthers. Adding Louis gives the Dolphins some much-needed speed and playmaking ability at the second level, and we're excited to see him fly around the field in coverage and run support.

Moore comes equipped with excellent ball skills and physicality at the catch point. He totaled 4 interceptions and 12 passes defended in his final season, showing a knack for making big plays in coverage. Moore gives the Dolphins another perimeter threat who can lock down outside targets and help balance a secondary that's been a little thin on playmakers.

Slaughter is a tough, smart center who's deceptively athletic for his large frame. He started all 13 games last season and was a team leader in the trenches, showing consistency in both run blocking and pass protection. Adding Slaughter gives Miami some much needed depth on the interior of the offensive line and is a plug and play fill-in for Aaron Brewer should Sully choose not to sign him long-term.

Wheatley is a versatile safety with excellent range and a nose for the ball. He's fast enough to cover deep and physical enough to support the run. He racked up 78 tackles, 3 interceptions, and 2 forced fumbles last year, giving the Dolphins a potential playmaker at a position that's been in need of some fresh blood.

Marshall is a smaller corner who thrives in man coverage and excels on special teams. He's quick, aggressive, and has a solid tape of making plays both in coverage and near the line of scrimmage. Marshall adds depth to the secondary and a guy who can contribute immediately in multiple phases of the game.

Green is a big-armed dual-threat QB with explosive athleticism and intriguing upside. He threw for over 3,000 yards and 28 touchdowns last season while rushing for 600+ yards, showing he can make plays with both his arm and legs. This is a developmental pick for the Dolphins, someone who could push Malik Willis in practice and maybe surprise down the line.
